text: Conclusions

This thesis set out to address a concrete and widely felt problem in the Egyptian healthcare market: the lab-testing sector is fragmented and opaque. Prices for an identical test vary widely between laboratories and are rarely published, patients have no neutral way to compare options or read other patients' experiences, results arrive as one-off PDF documents that cannot be trended over time, and there is no privacy-respecting mechanism for a patient's longitudinal history to follow them when they switch laboratories. Smaller laboratories, in turn, lack a low-cost storefront, scheduling, and results-delivery system, and the ecosystem as a whole lacks a trusted, curated intermediary that verifies that a laboratory is operationally complete before exposing it to patients.

In response, this project delivered **TesTly**, a curated, multi-actor medical lab-testing marketplace that connects patients with accredited laboratories to discover tests, book them in one of three fulfilment modes (lab visit, home sample collection, or home test kit), pay online or in cash, and receive results as both an authoritative PDF and structured, queryable data. The system was realised as a coherent, end-to-end platform in which a patient can move from an AI-assisted conversation to a compared, booked, paid, delivered, and trended test, while a laboratory can move from a gated registration to a fully operational storefront, all on one platform governed by an administrator.
A defining characteristic of the work is its technical breadth across four distinct layers of a modern software system, each built with an industry-standard stack:

items: **Backend.** A NestJS 11 REST API backed by Prisma 5 and PostgreSQL, exposing modules for authentication, the public marketplace, bookings and payments, patient and laboratory workspaces, results, clinical normalisation, administration, and the conversational endpoints. Business logic is protected by a layered guard pipeline (`JwtAuthGuard` to `RolesGuard` to `LabActiveGuard` to per-service ownership checks).

**Web application.** A Next.js 16 / React 19 single-page application that serves an unauthenticated public storefront alongside role-specific dashboards for the patient, laboratory staff, and administrator, styled with Tailwind CSS 4 and visualised with Recharts.

**Mobile application.** A native Flutter (Dart) application for iOS and Android serving the patient and laboratory roles, using Riverpod for state management, a Dio client with silent refresh-token rotation, biometric unlock, and on-device PDF rendering.

**Artificial intelligence.** An agentic health assistant powered by Google Gemini (`gemini-2.5-flash`) that streams replies over Server-Sent Events and runs a server-side function-calling loop grounded in the live catalogue, complemented by a deterministic, non-LLM guided-FAQ bot for cheap, common questions.

caption: The TesTly system architecture: two clients (web and mobile) over a single NestJS API, fronting PostgreSQL and four external services.

text: Beyond breadth, the platform exhibits three architectural signatures that recur across every subsystem. First, **layered, defence-in-depth security** appropriate to medical data: bcrypt password hashing, short-lived access JWTs paired with SHA-256-hashed, rotating refresh tokens, dual token transport (an `httpOnly` cookie for the web and a secure-storage Bearer token for mobile), the `SameSite=None; Secure` and same-origin `/api` rewrite combination that makes cross-site authentication work, stacked role and lab-active guards, and ownership-checked, authenticated PDF downloads with magic-byte upload validation. Second, a **clinical-normalisation engine** that resolves each laboratory's differently named and differently united analytes into a canonical, comparable form, enabling cross-lab health trends while a patient-controlled privacy toggle (`SAME_LAB_ONLY` versus `FULL_HISTORY_AUTHORIZED`) governs exactly what a laboratory may see. Third, **graceful, configuration-driven flexibility**: pluggable local-disk or S3 storage, optional push and AI that degrade to no-ops when unconfigured, one API serving two clients, and a schema that runs identically on local Docker Postgres or serverless Neon.

The administrative onboarding gate deserves particular emphasis as a contribution to the trust problem the project targets. An administrator cannot activate a laboratory until it satisfies five readiness requirements (phone, accreditation, turnaround, at least one active test, and at least one active slot); activation is refused with an explicit list of missing requirements otherwise. This guarantees that every bookable laboratory exposed to patients is operationally complete, directly addressing the absence of a trusted, curated intermediary identified in the problem statement.

In summary, TesTly demonstrates that a single, version-locked monorepo can deliver a production-shaped, multi-platform marketplace whose intelligence, grounding, and security all reside in a shared backend, while two thin client renderers and an agentic assistant present that capability to end users. The project met its objective of turning a fragmented, opaque lab-testing market into a transparent, comparison-first, continuity-preserving experience for patients and a complete back-office for laboratories.
Future Work

While TesTly is functionally complete as a graduation project, several concrete enhancements would raise it toward a production-grade commercial service. The following directions are ordered roughly from the most immediately actionable to the most ambitious.
Production Payment Integration

The current online payment path uses a simulated gateway (`POST /bookings/:id/demo-online-payment`) that sets the payment status without contacting a real provider. The clearest next step is to integrate a production payment processor such as PayMob, which is the standard gateway for the Egyptian market. This entails replacing the demo endpoint with a real charge-and-callback flow, handling asynchronous webhooks to reconcile `Paid` and `Failed` states, securing the refund path that already exists for cancellations, and adding reconciliation and idempotency safeguards around the existing booking transaction.
Automated Lab-Report OCR and Parsing

Today, structured observations that feed the cross-lab trend engine are entered through the laboratory workspace and upserted via `PUT /lab/results/:bookingId/structured`. A high-value enhancement is an automated pipeline that applies optical character recognition (OCR) and parsing to the uploaded result PDF, extracting analyte names, values, units, and reference ranges directly and feeding them into the existing `ClinicalNormalizationService` for canonical-marker resolution and unit conversion. This would dramatically reduce manual data entry for laboratories and increase the proportion of results that contribute to trends rather than remaining PDF-only.
Full Arabic Localization

The mobile application already supports English and Arabic with right-to-left layout via `flutter_localizations`, and the AI system prompt mirrors the user's language. Extending complete Arabic localization, including right-to-left support, to the web application would make the platform fully accessible to the broader Egyptian audience and bring web-mobile parity to the internationalisation story.
Real-Time Chat with Laboratories

The system currently communicates booking and result events to patients through push notifications and status timelines. Adding a real-time, two-way chat channel between a patient and the laboratory handling their booking, for example to clarify preparation instructions or coordinate a home collection, would close the communication loop. Because the agentic assistant already establishes a server-orchestrated streaming pattern, this feature could reuse much of the existing transport and persistence design.
Insurance Integration

A natural commercial extension is integration with health-insurance providers, allowing a patient to attach a policy, check coverage for a given test, and have eligible costs settled directly rather than paid out of pocket. This would require new data models for policies and claims, coordination with insurer APIs, and pricing logic that distinguishes the patient's co-payment from the insured portion at booking time.
Expanded AI Capabilities

The agentic assistant is deliberately bounded today, exposing two grounded tools (`find_labs` and `search_tests`) over the public catalogue and explicitly forbidden from interpreting an individual patient's results. Future work could, with appropriate clinical safeguards and explicit consent, allow the assistant to reason over a patient's own normalised trend data to offer educational context, add tools that initiate a booking directly from the conversation, and broaden the catalogue grounding to richer test metadata, all while preserving the existing non-diagnosis and emergency-escalation guardrails.
Analytics for Laboratories

Laboratory staff already receive an aggregated dashboard (totals, completed bookings, pending results, a revenue estimate, capacity-usage percentage, and top tests). Deeper analytics would extend this into longitudinal reporting: demand forecasting, time-series revenue and booking trends, competitive price benchmarking against other laboratories offering the same test, and capacity-planning insights, giving smaller laboratories the kind of business intelligence that the platform's data uniquely makes possible.
Deeper Accreditation and Verification

The administrative onboarding gate currently records accreditation as a self-declared field among the five readiness requirements. A stronger verification process would validate accreditation against authoritative external registries, support document upload and review, and potentially introduce periodic re-verification and tiered trust badges. This would harden the curation guarantee that underpins patient trust in the marketplace.
Continuous Integration and Delivery

Finally, although the project carries substantial per-layer test suites (Jest with Supertest on the backend, Playwright role journeys on the web, and the most extensive suite of all, the Flutter unit tests on mobile), there is no automated CI pipeline; quality is currently governed by a manual deployment checklist. Wiring these existing suites into an automated pipeline (for example, GitHub Actions) that enforces typecheck and test passes before deployment would be the single highest-leverage step for engineering rigour and the natural foundation for all the feature work above.
